note_data.title: People in Business
keyPoints: The aim of a business in the private sector is to survive by making a profit
Primary production involves acquiring raw materials such as metals, coal, oil, rubber, foodstuffs and fish - sometimes known as extractive production
Secondary production is the manufacturing and assembly process, converting raw materials into components and assembling products
Tertiary production refers to commercial services that support production and distribution including insurance, transport, advertising, warehousing, retail, teaching and health care
All limited companies are incorporated, meaning they can sue or own assets in their own right and have limited liability
A public limited company (PLC) can sell its shares on the Stock Market, while a private limited company (Ltd) cannot
Sole traders do not have a separate legal existence from their owner and have unlimited liability for the firm's debts
Partnerships are businesses owned by two or more people with a deed of partnership contract drawn up
Private limited companies are easy and inexpensive to set up but lack capital due to no share issue
Public limited companies can raise large amounts of capital from share issue and benefit from economies of scale

definitions.definition: A percentage of overall sales in an industry
An independent check on the accounts of the company
Where the sole trader can be forced to sell personal assets to cover any business debts
Their losses are limited to the amount they invested in the business

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Name or select from given information. No explanation required.
Give main features or general principles. Brief explanation needed.

modelAnswer.question: Explain two ways a business could motivate its employees. [6 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: One way is to offer performance-related pay. This means employees receive extra payment based on how well they perform their job. This motivates employees because they have a financial incentive to work harder and more effectively. Another way is job enlargement. This means giving employees more tasks of a similar level to make their job more varied. This motivates employees because it makes their work less boring and repetitive, increasing job satisfaction.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Explanation of why it motivates
Mark 6: Explanation of why it motivates

commonMistakes: Confusing that PLC shares can be sold on Stock Market while Ltd shares cannot
Not understanding that sole traders have unlimited liability while limited companies have limited liability
Mixing up the three stages of production - primary (extractive), secondary (manufacturing) and tertiary (services)
Forgetting that limited companies must indicate their status using PLC or Ltd in their name
examinerTips: Remember that both types of limited company must audit their accounts and have them available for inspection
Note that businesses gain larger market-share by increasing sales against competitors, which may involve reducing prices
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Maslow's hierarchy of needs pyramid showing physiological needs at bottom, then safety, social, esteem, and self-actualization at top. Essential for explaining different motivation factors.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain two advantages and two disadvantages of piece rate payment system for a manufacturing business
N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on defining job rotation as a method of training
M/J 2022 P2 Q3b (4 marks) — candidates had to analyze why a business might choose internal recruitment over external recruitment
N 2021 P2 Q5a (8 marks) — students needed to evaluate whether financial or non-financial motivation would be more effective for office workers
M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — identifying which payment method would best motivate sales staff in a car dealership

workedExample.question: Explain two advantages to a business of using job rotation as a training method. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define job rotation - moving employees between different jobs/departments to gain varied experience
Step 2: Identify first advantage with clear business benefit - creates multi-skilled workforce
Step 3: Explain how this helps the business - can cover for absent staff, increases flexibility
Step 4: Identify second advantage - reduces employee boredom/increases motivation
Step 5: Explain the business benefit - higher productivity, lower staff turnover costs
workedExample.answer: Job rotation involves moving employees between different roles to gain varied experience. One advantage is that it creates a multi-skilled workforce, meaning employees can cover different positions when colleagues are absent, giving the business greater flexibility in staffing. Another advantage is that it reduces employee boredom by providing variety in their work, which increases motivation and leads to higher productivity and lower staff turnover costs for the business.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for each advantage: 1 mark for identifying the advantage, 1 mark for explaining how it benefits the business specifically

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused motivation theories and failed to apply them to the business context given' — always relate Maslow's hierarchy or Herzberg's theory to the specific workplace scenario in the question

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often listed recruitment methods without explaining why internal or external would be better for the situation described' — always justify your choice of recruitment method based on the business's specific needs, size, or industry

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates frequently mixed up different payment systems and their effects' — clearly distinguish between time rate (fixed wage), piece rate (per unit produced), commission (percentage of sales), and profit sharing when discussing motivation
note_data.keyDiagram: Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs pyramid with 5 levels from bottom to top: Physiological needs (food, shelter, basic salary), Safety needs (job security, safe working conditions), Social needs (teamwork, belonging to groups), Esteem needs (recognition, status, promotion), Self-actualization (creativity, personal growth, challenging work). Students must be able to draw this pyramid, label each level, give workplace examples for each level, and explain how businesses can satisfy each need to motivate employees.
note_data.quickRecap: Private sector businesses aim to survive by making a profit through primary, secondary and tertiary production stages. Business structures include sole traders (unlimited liability), partnerships (shared ownership), and limited companies (limited liability). Public limited companies can sell shares on the Stock Market while private limited companies cannot, but both have separate legal existence from their owners.
